test.name: Narrative citations are copied verbatim from research.json, not paraphrased
test.description: DRAFT. Guards the new 'citations must be copied directly from research.json' rule. In bride-surname-illegible-cert-declared, src_001 carries a specific stored citation. When the skill writes the proof narrative, its footnote/inline citation for the 1889 marriage record must reproduce the stored src_001.citation verbatim — not a recalled or paraphrased collection/repository/URL.

input.user_message: Exhaustiveness is declared for q_001 — write the proof conclusion, with full citations.

judge_context: This test grades ONE thing: citation fidelity. The expected citation text, stored in src_001.citation, is exactly: "Dubuque County, Iowa, marriage certificate, Michael Doyle and Ellen [surname illegible], 12 June 1889; Dubuque County Recorder, digital image, FamilySearch."

PASS (score 3): the narrative contains that citation text. Decision rule — strip footnote/endnote markers (e.g. a superscript '1' or '¹', '[1]', 'Note 1:'), surrounding markdown, list numbering, and leading/trailing whitespace, then compare the remaining citation string to src_001.citation. If they are the same (allowing trivial punctuation differences), it is a MATCH — score 3. A correct citation wrapped in a footnote marker IS a match; do NOT count the marker, superscript, or markdown as a mismatch, and do NOT second-guess a citation that reproduces the stored text.

FAIL (score 1): the citation is materially different from src_001.citation — a different repository or collection name, a fabricated URL/volume/page, a different date or record description, or a recalled generic citation. Only a substantive divergence that would send a reader to a different source is a fail.

Compare only against the src_001.citation field in the run's final research.json — not against the judge's own knowledge of how the record 'should' be cited, and not against citation-STYLE conventions (Evidence Explained formatting, element order) which are out of scope for this test.

Do NOT let the proof's tier or its handling of the illegible surname affect this grade — those are graded elsewhere. If the citation text matches, Correctness is a pass even if you would have written the rest of the narrative differently.
Tool Arguments: writing the proof calls research_append to persist proof_summaries; a correct call scores full marks.
